title: Section 3: The Brain and Drug Effects
content: In this section: brain structure, brain functions, drug effects on the nervous system, tetanus, and how the nervous and endocrine systems compare.
image: Labelled diagram of the human brain showing all five main regions

title: The Brain: Five Main Parts
content: The brain has five key structures — each with a distinct function. You need to know all five by name.
image: Brain diagram with five regions colour-coded and labelled

title: Forebrain vs Hindbrain
content: The brain is divided into two broad regions: the forebrain and the hindbrain.
image: Split brain diagram — forebrain highlighted in one colour, hindbrain in another

title: Cerebral Hemispheres — Functions
content: The cerebral hemispheres (cerebrum) form the largest part of the brain and control conscious activity.
image: Diagram showing the cerebral hemispheres at the top of the brain

title: Hypothalamus and Pituitary Gland
content: These two forebrain structures work closely together to regulate the body's internal environment and hormone output.
bullets: Hypothalamus: regulates temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep
image: Close-up diagram showing hypothalamus and pituitary gland location in the brain

title: Did You Know?
content: The pituitary gland is about the size of a pea — yet it controls growth, reproduction, and the activity of several other glands in the body. Small but powerful.
image: Size comparison: pea next to an outline of the human body with arrows to glands

title: Cerebellum — Function
content: The cerebellum is located at the back of the brain and is part of the hindbrain.
image: Diagram highlighting the cerebellum at the rear of the brain

title: Medulla Oblongata — Function
content: The medulla oblongata is at the base of the brain and connects to the spinal cord. It controls vital automatic functions.
image: Diagram showing medulla oblongata at base of brain, connecting to spinal cord

title: Think About It
content: If the medulla oblongata controls breathing and heartbeat automatically — does that mean you can breathe even when you are not thinking about it? Yes. That is exactly why you breathe while you sleep.
image: Simple illustration of a sleeping person with a small brain diagram inset

title: Effects of Drugs: Three Categories
content: The syllabus classifies drugs into three categories based on how they affect the nervous system.
bullets: Stimulants — speed up the nervous system
image: Three-column table showing the three drug categories with examples under each

title: Depressants
content: Depressants slow down nervous system activity, making responses slower and less coordinated.
image: Diagram showing a slowed nerve signal, with a downward arrow on reaction speed

title: Stimulants
content: Stimulants speed up activity in the nervous system, producing increased alertness and physical activity.
image: Diagram showing accelerated nerve signals with upward arrows on heart rate and alertness

title: Hallucinogens and Tetanus
content: Hallucinogens distort how the brain processes information. Tetanus is a bacterial infection that damages the nervous system directly.
image: Split slide: hallucinogen effect on perception (cerebral hemispheres) vs tetanus toxin pathway to nerve tissue

title: Nervous System vs Endocrine System
content: Both systems coordinate the body's responses — but they work differently. You must be able to compare and contrast them.
bullets: Endocrine: hormones in blood, slower, longer-lasting
Hypothalamus and pituitary gland link both systems
image: Side-by-side comparison table: Nervous System vs Endocrine System — signal type, speed, duration, example organ

title: Section 3 Summary
content: You have covered all the key content for the brain and drug effects. Here is what to remember.
bullets: 5 brain parts: cerebral hemispheres, cerebellum, hypothalamus, pituitary gland, medulla oblongata
Depressants increase reaction time; stimulants increase motor activity and heart rate; hallucinogens distort judgment
Tetanus toxin damages brain cells and impairs nerve tissue
Nervous system: fast signals; endocrine system: slower hormones — linked via hypothalamus and pituitary gland
image: Summary graphic with all five brain regions and three drug categories in one visual
